headline: Bacteria's Cancer-Fighting Secret Finally Decoded
meta_description: Researchers crack nature's recipe for building powerful cancer drugs, opening doors to targeted therapies inspired by bacterial enzymes.
summary_short: Scientists have solved a decades-old mystery about how bacteria naturally manufacture multiple versions of powerful anti-cancer drugs.
body:
For decades, researchers watched bacteria perform an elegant trick—manufacturing multiple versions of potent cancer drugs—without understanding how. Now, scientists at the University of Warwick have finally cracked the code.

Published in Nature Communications, the breakthrough reveals that tiny molecular regions called "docking domains" act as connectors, allowing bacterial enzymes to communicate and cooperate. These flexible connectors enable bacteria to produce a variety of related drug molecules while maintaining the precision needed for effectiveness.

The discovery focuses on a class of medicines called HDAC inhibitors, which work by blocking enzymes that control which genes are switched on or off inside cells. Romidepsin (Istodax), an FDA-approved treatment for T-cell lymphomas, belongs to this family. The study also identified how a related compound called FR-901375—known for decades but mysteriously produced—finally fits into nature's toolkit.

Dr. Munro Passmore, lead researcher, explained: "This work finally cracks that code. We've identified how the different enzymes communicate and cooperate to produce these drug variants."

The implications are profound. By reverse-engineering nature's logic, scientists can now design synthetic pathways that generate new anti-cancer candidates with superior potency, improved selectivity, and fewer side effects—precisely where new treatments are urgently needed.
